pattern: Waviness only ever looks at three neighboring digits at a time, so summing it across an entire range is just a matter of walking each number's digits once and adding up every interior zig-zag it finds.
hints: A digit only counts toward waviness if it is strictly bigger than both neighbors, or strictly smaller than both -- nothing else about the number matters for that check.
So for one number, you can compute its own waviness in a single left-to-right scan of its digit string, checking each interior position against the digit before and after it.
Since the range is small enough to enumerate directly, add up every number's own waviness from num1 to num2 one at a time.

edge_inputs.description: a single number with no peaks or valleys, too short
a single three-digit number that is itself a peak
a single three-digit number that is itself a valley
smallest possible range, num1 equals num2 at the minimum bound
the entire allowed range from smallest to largest
a number with multiple interior peaks and valleys back to back
a range crossing a round-number boundary where digit lengths change
monotonic digits produce zero waviness despite being multi-digit

stress.description: random sub-ranges across the full constraint span
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    a = rng.randint(1, hi)
    b = rng.randint(a, hi)
    return [a, b]
